1: Ps 147: Triple-Movement Praise Psalm — YHWH's Cosmic Care and Covenant Particularity. Ps 147 has a distinctive three-movement structure, each beginning with a praise summons: 'Praise the LORD!' (v1), 'Sing to the LORD with thanksgiving' (v7), 'Praise the LORD, O Jerusalem!' (v12). The psalm's theological genius is its juxtaposition of the cosmic (YHWH determines the number of the stars, v4) with the intimate (YHWH heals the brokenhearted, v3) — the same God who maintains the stellar order also counts and names each star and each wound. The closing verses (vv19-20) make explicit that YHWH's word and statutes belong particularly to Israel — a universal creation-theology climaxing in covenant particularity. 'Praise the LORD! For it is good to sing praises to our God; for it is pleasant, and a song of praise is fitting' — the triple justification for praise: good (ki tov), pleasant (ki naim), fitting (tehillah navah).
2: 'The LORD builds up Jerusalem; he gathers the outcasts of Israel' — 'builds up Jerusalem' (YHWH boneh Yerushalaim) — the post-exilic return as divine city-building: YHWH himself is the builder who restores what the Babylonians destroyed (cf. Psalms 51:18; Nehemiah 2:17-18). 'Gathers the outcasts of Israel' (nidchei Yisrael yekanneys) — the scattered diaspora gathered: the technical term nidach (the outcast/banished) refers to those expelled from the land (Deuteronomy 30:4; Isaiah 11:12; Psalms 56:8). The Psalm's opening establishes the post-exilic context: YHWH is actively rebuilding and regathering.
3: 'He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds' — 'heals the brokenhearted' (harophe lishvure lev) — YHWH as the divine physician of interior wounds: not the wounds of battle but the wounds of grief, exile, loss. 'Binds up their wounds' (umechabesh le'atzeivotam) — the bandaging (chabash) of wounds is the medical image: careful, attentive binding. The same God who gathers the scattered exiles (v2) also heals the interior damage that exile has caused. The verse bridges the national (regathering) and the personal (healing) dimensions of restoration.
4: 'He determines the number of the stars; he gives to all of them their names' — 'determines the number of the stars' (moneh mispar lakochavim) — YHWH counts the uncountable: the ancient Near East saw the stars as beyond human enumeration (Genesis 15:5: 'Look toward heaven, and number the stars, if you are able to number them'). 'Gives to all of them their names' (lekullam shemot yiqra) — naming as an act of sovereignty (Genesis 2:19-20: Adam named the animals as an expression of human dominion over creation). YHWH's naming of every star establishes his sovereign knowledge of every created thing. The same God who numbers and names every star also counts and knows every wound (v3).
5: 'Great is our Lord, and abundant in power; his understanding is beyond measure' — 'abundant in power' (rav koach) — the greatness of YHWH is measured in power — not the abstract omnipotence of philosophy but the covenant power demonstrated in Exodus, conquest, and return from exile. 'His understanding is beyond measure' (litevunato ein mispar, his understanding has no number) — in contrast to the stars whose number YHWH knows (v4), YHWH's own understanding is innumerable, incalculable. The asymmetry is the point: what YHWH counts cannot count YHWH.
6: 'The LORD lifts up the humble; he casts the wicked to the ground' — the divine reversal formula (cf. Psalms 113:7-8; 1 Samuel 2:7-8): the humble (anavim) lifted, the wicked (resha'im) cast down. The anavim (the poor, humble, meek) are the characteristic recipients of divine grace throughout the Psalter (Psalms 9:12; Psalms 22:26; Psalms 37:11; Psalms 149:4). The reversal is not a general principle but a covenant promise: YHWH has aligned himself with the humble.
7: 'Sing to the LORD with thanksgiving; make melody to our God on the lyre!' — the second movement begins: anu laYHWH betodah (sing/answer to YHWH with thanksgiving). The todah (thanksgiving) vocabulary connects to the thanksgiving sacrifice (cf. Psalms 116:17) — music as the vocal complement to the sacrificial act. The lyre (kinnor) was the primary instrument of Levitical praise (1 Chronicles 25:1-6; Psalms 33:2).
8: 'He covers the heavens with clouds; he prepares rain for the earth; he makes grass grow on the hills' — the creation-care sequence: clouds → rain → grass. The hydrological cycle as divine providence. Elijah's prayer for rain (1 Kings 18:41-45) and the covenant blessings of Leviticus 26:4 and Deuteronomy 28:12 establish rain as the most fundamental sign of YHWH's active provision. The grass on the hills is the ultimate beneficiary — the transformation from bare hill to covered pasture through divine precipitation.
9: 'He gives to the beasts their food, and to the young ravens that cry' — 'young ravens that cry' (livenei orev asher yiqra'u) — the young ravens' cry is answered by divine provision (Job 38:41: 'Who provides for the raven its prey, when its young ones cry to God?'). The raven was an unclean bird (Leviticus 11:15), ritually excluded from Israel's sacrificial system — yet YHWH provides for it. Jesus echoes this in Luke 12:24 ('Consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap, they have neither storehouse nor barn, and yet God feeds them').
10: 'His delight is not in the strength of the horse, nor his pleasure in the legs of a man' — YHWH's non-delight in military power: 'the strength of the horse' (koach hassus) — the warhorse as the supreme symbol of military might (Psalms 20:7; Psalms 33:17; Isaiah 31:1). 'Nor his pleasure in the legs of a man' (shiqei haish) — human athletic or military prowess. The contrast with vv8-9: YHWH delights in feeding ravens, not in equipping armies. The covenant military theology: Israel's security comes from covenant trust, not from cavalry (Deuteronomy 17:16: the king was prohibited from multiplying horses).
11: 'But the LORD takes pleasure in those who fear him, in those who hope in his steadfast love' — the positive counterpart to v10: YHWH's delight (rotzeh) is in 'those who fear him' (yare'av) and 'those who hope in his steadfast love' (hameyachalim lechasdo). Two covenant postures: fear (the covenant reverence of Psalms 111:10) and hope in chesed (the trust that YHWH's loyal love will prove reliable). These are what YHWH values more than military hardware.
12: 'Praise the LORD, O Jerusalem! Praise your God, O Zion!' — the third movement: 'Praise the LORD, O Jerusalem!' (shabchi YHWH Yerushalaim) — the city itself summoned to praise. Zion and Jerusalem are addressed as if they are covenant partners capable of worship. The personification of the city as worshiper reflects the prophetic tradition (Isaiah 40:9; Psalms 52:1-2; Psalms 54:1) of addressing Zion as a person whose fortunes have changed.
13: 'For he strengthens the bars of your gates; he blesses your children within you' — 'strengthens the bars of your gates' (ki chizeq berichei she'arayich) — the city's gate-bars are the primary urban defense mechanism in the ancient world. YHWH as the one who fortifies them. 'Blesses your children within you' (berach banayich beqirbech) — the blessing of the next generation within the secure city. The physical security (gate-bars) and the biological future (children) are both YHWH's gift.
14: 'He makes peace in your borders; he fills you with the finest of the wheat' — 'peace in your borders' (hasam gevulech shalom) — the covenant blessing of Leviticus 26:6 ('I will give peace in the land') applied to post-exilic Jerusalem. 'The finest of the wheat' (chelev chittim yasbiech) — the fat/best of the wheat: the covenant abundance that Psalms 81:16 promised ('I would feed you with the finest of the wheat'). Peace and abundance are paired covenant blessings.
15: 'He sends out his command to the earth; his word runs swiftly' — 'his word runs swiftly' (ad-mehera yaruts devaro) — the divine Word personified as a swift messenger. The creation-by-word theology (Gen 1; Psalms 33:6,9: 'he spoke and it came to be') is now applied to ongoing governance: YHWH's commands continue to run through the created order, maintaining it. Isaiah 55:11 ('my word that goes out from my mouth shall not return to me empty') develops this theme. John 1:1-14 (the Word who 'was God' and 'became flesh') takes the personification of the divine word to its christological culmination.
16: 'He gives snow like wool; he scatters frost like ashes' — the winter weather phenomena as divine actions: snow 'like wool' (hakkefar kaefer) — the visual comparison of white snow to white wool fleece. Frost 'like ashes' (kepher kaepher yefazer) — the scattered appearance of frost across a surface. These meteorological phenomena are not accidents of nature but YHWH's active sending. Isaiah 1:18 uses 'white as snow' as the image of divine forgiveness.
17: 'He hurls down his crystals of ice like crumbs; who can stand before his cold?' — 'crystals of ice like crumbs' (karcho kephitim yashlich) — hail or ice-pellets thrown like bread-crumbs. 'Who can stand before his cold?' (liphnei qarato mi ya'amod) — the rhetorical question: no creature can withstand the divine cold. The natural world's extremes are expressions of YHWH's sovereign power, applied to praise rather than threat.
18: 'He sends out his word and melts them; he makes his wind blow and the waters flow' — the reversal: 'sends out his word and melts them' (yishlach devaro veyamsem) — the same divine word that sent the snow (v16) and ice (v17) now melts them. 'His wind blow and the waters flow' (yashev rucho yizzelu mayim) — the spring thaw as divine word: when YHWH speaks, the frozen season ends. The ruach (wind/spirit) of YHWH that moved over the primordial waters (Genesis 1:2) now moves over the ice to produce flow.
19: 'He declares his word to Jacob, his statutes and rules to Israel' — the transition from creation (vv4-18) to covenant: 'declares his word to Jacob' (magid devarav leYaakov) — the same divine Word (dabar) that runs through creation (v15) and melts the ice (v18) is also the Torah given to Israel. The covenant people receive the creative word as their law. The equation of creation-word and covenant-word is explicit: Torah is not separate from creation but the same divine speech applied to the covenant community's life.
20: 'He has not dealt thus with any other nation; they do not know his rules. Praise the LORD!' — the psalm's climax: the particularism that the universal creation-theology leads to. 'He has not dealt thus with any other nation' (lo asah chen lechol goy) — YHWH's self-disclosure through law and covenant is Israel's unique privilege. No other nation has the Torah. 'They do not know his rules' (umishpatim bal yeda'um) — the nations' ignorance is not contemptible but simply factual: the covenant statutes are Israel's inheritance alone. The Psalm that opened with universal praise (vv1-6) closes with the covenant's particularity: creation is universal; covenant is particular. The 'Hallelujah' returns.
